content.text: What it is

Dashboard review and ownership standards are the policies, processes, and tooling that ensure every production dashboard has a named owner, goes through a review gate before promotion, and is actively maintained throughout its lifetime. In practice this means three things are defined and enforced: who is responsible for a dashboard (ownership), how a new or changed dashboard gets approved (review process), and how dashboards are kept accurate over time (cadence and freshness checks).
Ownership is recorded inside the dashboard JSON itself as custom metadata tags — typically
owner
team
, and
last-reviewed-date
— and in a
CODEOWNERS

file that maps the dashboard's directory path to the GitHub teams responsible for reviewing changes. The review process is PR-based: any change to a dashboard file requires approval from the listed owners before it can merge. Freshness is maintained through automated checks that detect panels returning no data, stale datasource references, and duplicate dashboards.
Why it matters

Dashboards rot. Over the six to twelve months following a dashboard's initial creation, the service it monitors changes: metrics are renamed or removed, data sources are migrated to new clusters, service endpoints are restructured, and alert thresholds become outdated. Without active ownership, these changes accumulate invisibly. The dashboard continues to load, but panels show 'No Data', thresholds no longer reflect current SLOs, and the queries hit deprecated data sources that return misleading partial data.

A dashboard that appears to function but actually shows stale or incorrect data is worse than a broken dashboard. A broken dashboard is visibly broken — an on-call engineer will investigate. A silently misleading dashboard causes an engineer to act on bad data during an incident, potentially delaying diagnosis or causing a harmful intervention. Dashboard ownership standards exist specifically to prevent this failure mode.
Benefits
Accountability: every production dashboard has a named team. When a panel is wrong or a threshold is outdated, there is no ambiguity about who investigates and fixes it.
Quality gate: the PR review process catches structurally broken panels, missing owner tags, and queries that reference deprecated metrics before the dashboard reaches production.
Reduced alert fatigue: dashboards are kept current, so on-call engineers trust what they see. When engineers trust their dashboards they act more quickly and with more confidence.

Visible health of the observability programme: the freshness check output gives the platform team a fleet-wide view of which dashboards are stale, which have 'No Data' panels, and which have been abandoned.
Compliance evidence: version-controlled dashboards with review history and owner metadata provide an audit trail for regulatory or internal compliance requirements.
Trade-offs

Process overhead: every dashboard change requires a PR and reviewer approval. For small teams with infrequent dashboard changes, this is negligible. For large teams making daily dashboard updates, it can become a bottleneck if CODEOWNERS paths are over-specified or reviewers are unavailable.

Quarterly review fatigue: if an organisation has hundreds of dashboards, scheduling quarterly reviews for all of them simultaneously creates a spike of review work. Stagger review deadlines by team or by service criticality.

False positives in freshness checks: a panel that legitimately has no data in the audit window (e.g., a guardrail panel that shows data only when a guardrail fires) will be flagged as stale. Freshness checks need an allowlist mechanism for known-sparse panels.
How it works
The review and ownership lifecycle has two phases:
Phase 1 — promotion gate: when a new dashboard or a change to an existing dashboard is proposed, it enters a PR. GitHub's
CODEOWNERS
file automatically requests review from the correct reviewers based on the file path. A CI job runs
grafana-dashboard-linter

and a custom ownership-tag validator against the changed dashboard JSON. The PR cannot merge until all three conditions are met: owner-tag present in the JSON, linter passes, and CODEOWNERS approval granted.

Phase 2 — ongoing maintenance: a weekly cron job queries the Grafana API for every dashboard panel, checks whether each panel returned data in the last 24 hours, detects stale datasource references, and flags panels with no data for owner notification. Quarterly, an automated issue is opened in the owning team's repository requesting re-attestation: the owner reviews the dashboard, updates the
last-reviewed-date
tag, and closes the issue. Dashboards with no response to the quarterly issue within 14 days are automatically moved to an archive folder.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Dashboard lifecycle with four phase boxes (Create, Lint/CI, PR Review, Deploy), automated freshness checks in production (no-data scan, stale datasource, duplicate detection, quarterly review gate), and three ownership tiers (primary, secondary, platform owner)

content.text: The ownership architecture has three tiers, each with a distinct responsibility scope:
Primary owner
: the team that deploys and operates the service. Responsible for keeping panels accurate, updating thresholds when SLOs change, and responding to quarterly review requests. Listed in the dashboard
tags
field as
owner:team-name
Secondary owner

: the on-call rotation that uses the dashboard at 2 AM. May be the same as the primary owner or a shared SRE rotation. Responsible for raising accuracy issues as bugs against the primary owner. Listed in the dashboard as
oncall:rotation-name
Platform owner

: the observability or platform engineering team. Reviews all new dashboards for template compliance and metric naming convention adherence. Does not own the content correctness of individual service dashboards — that is the primary owner's responsibility. Listed in CODEOWNERS for the global dashboards directory.
When to use it

From the first production dashboard. Retroactively adding ownership standards to a large fleet of dashboards is significantly harder than establishing them from the start. The CODEOWNERS file should be created alongside the first dashboard directory.

When you are operating dashboards-as-code. Ownership review is a natural PR gate when dashboards are committed to version control. Without VCS, you would need to use Grafana's built-in permissions, which are less auditable.
When dashboards are shared across teams. Single-team dashboards can rely on team norms. Cross-team shared dashboards need explicit ownership assignment and review process to avoid ownership vacuum.
How to implement
Establish a dashboard directory structure in the repository: one subdirectory per service under a top-level dashboards/ directory. This structure is what CODEOWNERS acts on.

Write a CODEOWNERS file mapping each dashboard subdirectory to the owning GitHub team. Add the platform observability team as a required reviewer for all files under dashboards/ to catch template-compliance issues.
Add a CI ownership-tag validator: a script that reads each modified dashboard JSON and verifies the presence of
owner
team
, and
last-reviewed-date
tags in the dashboard's
tags
array. Fail the CI job if any are absent.

Write a weekly freshness-check script that queries the Grafana API, retrieves all dashboards, and for each dashboard calls the Panel Data API to check whether at least one data point was returned in the past 24 hours. Emit findings to a Slack channel and open GitHub issues against the primary owner team for panels with no data.

Automate the quarterly review: use a cron-triggered GitHub Action that opens a review issue in the owning team's repository for each dashboard whose last-reviewed-date is more than 90 days ago. If the issue is not closed within 14 days, the dashboard is moved to an archive folder by an automated PR.
Examples
CODEOWNERS file for a dashboards-as-code repository:

# Global: platform observability team reviews all dashboard changes
/dashboards/                              @acme/observability-platform

# Service-specific ownership
/dashboards/loan-underwriting-agent/      @acme/lending-team @acme/observability-platform
/dashboards/fraud-signal-aggregator/      @acme/risk-team    @acme/observability-platform
/dashboards/customer-faq-bot/             @acme/cx-team      @acme/observability-platform
/dashboards/document-extraction/          @acme/data-team    @acme/observability-platform

# Shared / cross-service dashboards owned by platform team
/dashboards/fleet-overview/               @acme/observability-platform
Python script to audit dashboard freshness via the Grafana API:
Real-world example

An enterprise software company migrated its recommendation agent's metric names from a legacy naming scheme to OpenTelemetry semantic conventions over a two-month window. The data engineering team that performed the migration updated the agent's metric emission code and Prometheus recording rules but did not update the corresponding Grafana dashboard — because the dashboard had no named owner and no one was automatically notified of the metric rename.

For six weeks after the migration, the recommendation agent's dashboard showed all panels as 'No Data' for the new metric names and still displayed data on the old metric names (from a grace-period parallel export). During an incident with the agent's recommendation quality, the on-call engineer used the dashboard to conclude the agent was healthy based on the old metric export, missing a genuine quality degradation that the new metrics would have shown.

After implementing ownership standards with automated freshness checks, the same scenario would have triggered a 'No Data panel' alert to the dashboard owner within 24 hours of the metric rename, prompting an immediate dashboard update before any incident could be misread.
Common mistakes

Assigning ownership to a team alias that is no longer active. Team restructuring breaks CODEOWNERS rules silently — PRs get no reviewer and auto-merge or block forever. Audit CODEOWNERS entries quarterly against the current list of active GitHub teams.

Using calendar date as the only freshness signal. A dashboard with 'No Data' panels but a recent last-reviewed-date tag is not fresh — someone re-attested it without checking whether the data was actually flowing. Freshness checks must combine tag age and live panel-data coverage.

Making the platform team the sole reviewer. This creates a bottleneck and causes the platform team to become a de facto rubber-stamp approver because they lack domain context for every service. Use CODEOWNERS to require both the service team's approval and the platform team's approval.

Archiving instead of deleting stale dashboards. Archived dashboards can be mistakenly accessed during incidents. Prefer deletion with a six-month grace period in version control, so the dashboard can be recovered if needed but does not appear in Grafana search results.
Not handling panels that legitimately have no data. Guardrail panels, canary deployment panels, and rarely-fired alert panels will always fail a 24-hour data coverage check. Add a tag such as
sparse-panel:true
to suppress false-positive freshness alerts for these panels.
Best practices
Encode ownership in the dashboard JSON, not only in CODEOWNERS. The JSON travels with the dashboard if it is exported, imported, or backed up — CODEOWNERS does not. Both sources should agree.

Make the quarterly review lightweight. The review task should take under 15 minutes: open the dashboard, confirm all panels have data, confirm thresholds reflect current SLOs, update the last-reviewed-date tag, and merge the PR. A review that takes hours will be deferred and ignored.

Use GitHub issue templates for quarterly review requests. The issue should pre-fill the dashboard URL, owner team, and a checklist of review criteria. This reduces the cognitive load of the review and makes it consistently thorough.

Distinguish between review (confirming correctness) and update (changing the dashboard). A quarterly review that results in no changes is a successful review — silence or approval with no changes is the expected outcome for a well-maintained dashboard.

Track the freshness audit output over time. Publish a dashboard-of-dashboards metric: percentage of production dashboards with full data coverage. Make this metric visible to engineering leadership as an indicator of observability programme health.
Performance and scalability

The freshness check script queries the Grafana API once per dashboard. At 50 dashboards with an average of 15 panels each, the script issues 750 API calls, which at Grafana's typical response time of 50–200 ms per call takes one to three minutes — acceptable for a weekly cron job.
At 500+ dashboards (a large enterprise deployment), naive panel-by-panel polling is too slow. Two optimisations apply: use the Grafana
/api/ds/query

endpoint to batch multiple panel queries in a single HTTP request, reducing round trips; and cache datasource metadata to avoid repeated lookups when many panels reference the same datasource. At that scale, parallelising the API calls across a thread pool is also straightforward in Python with
concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor
Tooling and ecosystem
GitHub CODEOWNERS
: a native GitHub feature. A single file at
.github/CODEOWNERS
or
CODEOWNERS
at the repo root maps path globs to GitHub teams or users. Required reviewers block merges if the team has not approved.
grafana-dashboard-linter
: validates dashboard JSON schema and checks for common panel errors. Integrates as a GitHub Actions step.
Grafana HTTP API
: the
/api/search
and
/api/dashboards/uid/{uid}
endpoints are sufficient for freshness auditing. A service account with Viewer role is all that is needed — no admin access required.
GitHub Actions scheduled workflows
: run the freshness check and quarterly review scripts on a
schedule:
trigger. No additional infrastructure needed.
Comparison with alternatives
No ownership (implicit shared responsibility)

: common in organisations early in their observability journey. Dashboards exist but belong to no one. Rot accumulates invisibly and is discovered during incidents. Not viable in a multi-team environment.
Platform team owns all dashboards

: the observability team authors and maintains every dashboard for every service. This scales only to small organisations — the platform team quickly becomes the bottleneck for every metric rename or threshold change that service teams would prefer to handle themselves. Service teams lose the incentive to keep their dashboards accurate.
Tiered ownership with review gates (recommended)

: service teams own service-specific dashboards, the platform team owns shared and template compliance. Review gates are automated where possible. Freshness is maintained by the owning team, with automated prompts rather than manual tracking.
Security and reliability

The freshness audit script runs with a read-only Grafana service account. This matters because the script calls the Grafana API at scale — a write-capable token that is exfiltrated from CI could be used to modify or delete dashboards in production. Scope the service account to the minimum required: Viewer role on the dashboard folders the script audits.

Reliability of the review process itself depends on CODEOWNERS files being up to date. Stale CODEOWNERS entries (teams that have been renamed or dissolved) cause PR review requests to go to ghost teams, blocking merges indefinitely. Automate a monthly check that validates every team listed in CODEOWNERS still exists in the GitHub organisation.
Mental model
Think of a production dashboard as a
living contract

between the team that built the service and the engineers who will operate it during incidents. Like any contract, it requires two things to remain useful: a named party responsible for it, and periodic re-attestation that its terms still reflect reality.

The CODEOWNERS file is the contract ledger — it records who is responsible for each dashboard. The quarterly review is the contract renewal — the responsible party confirms the dashboard still accurately reflects the service. The freshness check is the contract auditor — an automated system that detects when the dashboard has drifted from reality between renewals. All three are needed. A contract with no party, no renewal, and no auditor is worth nothing when the moment of use arrives.
Key takeaways
Every production dashboard must have a named primary owner (service team), secondary owner (on-call rotation), and a platform owner (observability team) for template compliance.
Ownership is recorded in two places that must agree: the dashboard JSON tags (owner:, team:, last-reviewed:) and the repository CODEOWNERS file.
New dashboards require a PR-based review gate: CI validates owner tags and schema, CODEOWNERS enforces required approvals from service team and platform team.
Automated freshness checks run on a weekly cron: detect panels with no data in the past 24 hours, stale datasource references, and duplicate dashboards.
Quarterly review cadence is enforced by automation: an issue is opened against the owning team; non-response within 14 days triggers an automated archive PR.

A silently misleading dashboard — one that appears to work but shows stale or no data — is more dangerous than a visibly broken one. The entire point of ownership standards is to eliminate the silent-failure mode.
